title: Test case: Hardware generational shifts (TV ? smartphone ? EV ? self-driving)
body:
## Type
Generational hardware shift � tests the core loop across successive hardware generations, each solving the previous generation's constraint

## File
`06_tests/technology_cycles/hardware_generations.md`

## What Makes This Different
Unlike hype cycles, hardware generations do not crash � they are superseded. Each generation solves a constraint left by the previous one and becomes infrastructure for the next. The loop runs, but the low is not a crash � it is obsolescence.

## Generations to Map

### Communication / Screen
- **TV** ? solved: mass broadcast, shared information at scale
- **Fixed phone** ? solved: two-way real-time communication
- **Dumb phone** ? solved: communication untethered from location
- **Smartphone** ? solved: full internet + communication + compute in pocket
- **Next (wearables / AR?)** ? constraint being solved: screen as mediator between human and information

### Transport
- **Internal combustion car** ? solved: personal mobility at scale
- **EV** ? solving: fossil fuel dependency, software-defined transport
- **Self-driving** ? solving: human attention as the constraint on transport
- **Next (autonomous + connected fleet?)** ? constraint: ownership model and infrastructure coordination

## Pre-Commitment
- Each generation should show: builder class, scale increase, coordination breakdown (or obsolescence signal), coordinator emergence, infrastructure deposit
- Generation durations should compress � smartphone generation shorter than phone generation, EV faster than ICE dominance
- The constraint each generation solves should map to the dominant scarcity of its age

## Why This Test Matters
Hardware generations are slower-moving than hype cycles but more physically concrete. They test whether the loop holds even when there is no dramatic crash � just gradual supersession.

title: Test case: AI cycle (~2022�present)
body:
## Type
Technology hype cycle � tests core loop and compression model (ongoing)

## File
`06_tests/technology_cycles/ai_cycle.md`

## Constraint Solved
Execution � the gap between knowing what to do and being able to do it at scale.

## Pre-Commitment
- **High:** ~2022�2024. ChatGPT, GPT-4, Claude, Gemini. Massive VC investment, every company adding AI. Builders: OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind, Microsoft.
- **Breakdown (predicted):** ~2024�2025. Coordination failure beginning � regulation (EU AI Act), hallucination liability, job displacement backlash, model commoditisation, hype vs. reality gap.
- **Coordinators (emerging):** Governments (EU AI Act, US executive orders), enterprise governance frameworks, AI safety organisations.
- **Infrastructure left (predicted):** AI as baseline utility � like electricity or internet. Prompting and AI orchestration as assumed skill.
- **Cycle duration (predicted):** ~2�3 years

## Why This Is the Critical Test
This is the only cycle we can observe in real time. If it resolves in ~2�3 years, the compression model is confirmed at its predicted lower bound. If it drags to 5+ years, the lower bound may be higher than predicted.

## Compression Signal
Should be shorter than web3 (~4 years). At ~2�3 years, this would be the strongest compression signal yet � and would validate the predictions.md stage duration estimates.

title: Test case: Web 3.0 / crypto cycle (~2018�2022)
body:
## Type
Technology hype cycle � tests core loop and compression model

## File
`06_tests/technology_cycles/web3_cycle.md`

## Constraint Solved
Trust and ownership in digital systems � centralised platforms controlled identity, assets, and rules.

## Pre-Commitment
- **High:** ~2018�2021. ICO boom, DeFi, NFT explosion, metaverse hype. Builders: Ethereum ecosystem, Coinbase, OpenSea, Bored Apes.
- **Breakdown:** ~2022. Coordination failure � FTX collapse, Luna/Terra crash, NFT market collapse, regulatory crackdown. Trust implosion.
- **Coordinators:** SEC enforcement, exchange regulation, surviving protocols (Bitcoin, Ethereum), institutional custodians.
- **Infrastructure left:** Blockchain as a concept, tokenisation, digital ownership frameworks � still being absorbed.
- **Cycle duration:** ~4 years

## Compression Signal
Should be shorter than social media (~7 years). At ~4 years, strong compression signal.

title: Test case: Social media cycle (~2008�2018)
body:
## Type
Technology hype cycle � tests core loop and compression model

## File
`06_tests/technology_cycles/social_media_cycle.md`

## Constraint Solved
Human connection and attention at scale � the internet connected information but not people socially.

## Pre-Commitment
- **High:** ~2008�2016. Facebook, Twitter, Instagram, YouTube explosion. Builders: Zuckerberg, Systrom, Dorsey. Billions of users, massive ad revenue.
- **Breakdown:** ~2016�2018. Coordination failure � Cambridge Analytica, election interference, mental health crisis, polarisation. Trust collapse.
- **Coordinators:** GDPR, congressional hearings, platform policy changes, app stores as gatekeepers.
- **Infrastructure left:** Social graph, creator economy, influencer marketing become baseline. Attention economy fully established.
- **Cycle duration:** ~7 years

## Compression Signal
Should be shorter than the internet cycle (~10 years). At ~7 years, this would confirm compression.

title: Test case: Internet cycle (~1995�2005)
body:
## Type
Technology hype cycle � tests core loop and compression model at short timescale

## File
`06_tests/technology_cycles/internet_cycle.md`

## Constraint Solved
Information access and distribution � geography and gatekeepers limited who could publish, communicate, and find information.

## Pre-Commitment
- **High:** Builder phase ~1995�2000. VC flood, dot-com boom, rapid user adoption. Builders: Netscape, Amazon, Google, Yahoo.
- **Breakdown:** ~2000�2002. Coordination failure � markets mispriced risk, no revenue models, governance vacuum. Dot-com crash.
- **Coordinators:** Market correction, surviving platforms (Google, Amazon), emerging regulatory frameworks.
- **Infrastructure left:** The internet itself � email, search, e-commerce become baseline expectation.
- **Cycle duration:** ~7�10 years

## Why This Test Matters
The internet cycle is the reference point. Every subsequent cycle should be shorter. If it is not, the compression model fails.

title: Test case: Islamic civilisation and the Golden Age (~600�1400 CE)
body:
## Why This Test Matters
Islamic civilisation had its own distinct knowledge and coordination age � the House of Wisdom in Baghdad (~8th�13th century) � centuries before the Western framing places the Information Age. If the theory's ages are universal, the Islamic world should show a compressed version of the same loop running on a different timeline. If the ages are culturally specific to the West, Islamic history will show a different sequence entirely.

## File
`06_tests/islamic_golden_age.md`

## Pre-Commitment (Before Researching)
The theory predicts:
- The same scarcity progression should be traceable: food/land ? coordination/governance ? knowledge/information (earlier than the West)
- Builder classes should include scholars, engineers, mathematicians (the Golden Age as an Information Age analog)
- Coordination breakdown should be traceable (Mongol destruction of Baghdad 1258 as a coordination collapse event)
- The Islamic world may have *reached* the information scarcity constraint ~600 years before Europe � which would support the theory's universality but challenge the linear Western timeline

**Potential failure point:** If Islamic civilisation ran through the same ages but on a different clock, the theory's timeline (ages.md) needs to be reframed as one civilisation's path, not the universal path. This would be a significant structural revision � not a falsification, but a generalisation.

## Assertions to Score
A1 First principle / A2 Scarcity per age / A3 Builders / A4 Scale increase / A5 Coordination breakdown / A6 Coordinators / A7 Age compression

## Next Step
Fill `06_tests/islamic_golden_age.md` using the template. Pre-commit predictions before researching.

title: Test case: Sub-Saharan Africa (Mali Empire / Great Zimbabwe)
body:
## Why This Test Matters
Sub-Saharan Africa is the least represented civilisation in the theory's current evidence base. The Mali Empire (~1235�1600 CE) was one of the wealthiest and most organised states in the medieval world. Great Zimbabwe (~1100�1450 CE) built large-scale stone infrastructure with no written records. Testing here directly addresses the Western-centric bias in the theory's evidence.

## File
`06_tests/subsaharan_africa.md`

## Pre-Commitment (Before Researching)
The theory predicts:
- Dominant scarcities should be traceable: food/land ? trade route control ? labour/coordination
- Builder classes should be identifiable (architects of Great Zimbabwe, trade network builders in Mali)
- Coordination breakdowns should correspond to empire fragmentation
- The first principle should hold � expansion of the Mali Empire was driven by removing constraints on trade and resources

**Potential failure point:** Great Zimbabwe left no written records � the evidence base is archaeological, not textual. Age compression may be impossible to verify. This could reveal a methodological boundary: the theory's testability depends on documentation quality, which is unevenly distributed across civilisations.

## Assertions to Score
A1 First principle / A2 Scarcity per age / A3 Builders / A4 Scale increase / A5 Coordination breakdown / A6 Coordinators / A7 Age compression

## Next Step
Fill `06_tests/subsaharan_africa.md` using the template. Pre-commit predictions before researching.
